title: Pathogens — what causes infectious disease?
examTip: Pathogen questions are straightforward marks if you know the four types and how they cause disease. AQA regularly asks you to distinguish how bacteria and viruses cause symptoms — make sure you can explain both mechanisms clearly.
introTips: You're about to learn why you feel ill — not just 'germs got in', but exactly what different microorganisms do to your cells and why that causes symptoms.
There are four types of pathogen to know: bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists. Most exam questions focus on bacteria and viruses, so those mechanisms matter most.
Once you understand how pathogens spread, you can work out how to stop them — which is exactly what AQA asks you to do.
intro: Every time you've had a fever, a rash, or food poisoning, a microorganism was responsible. But 'microorganism' covers a huge range of things — some of them kill your cells directly, others poison you with toxins, some hijack your own DNA. Understanding the difference is what turns a vague answer into a full-mark answer. This lesson gives you the four pathogen types, the two main mechanisms of disease, and the routes they travel to reach you.

keyPoints.heading: What a pathogen is — and the four types
keyPoints.content: A pathogen is any microorganism that causes infectious disease — a disease that can be passed from one individual to another. There are four types AQA expects you to know: bacteria (e.g. Salmonella, gonorrhoea), viruses (e.g. measles, HIV, tobacco mosaic virus), fungi (e.g. athlete's foot, rose black spot), and protists (e.g. the malaria parasite Plasmodium). Note that not all microorganisms are pathogens — many bacteria are harmless or even beneficial. Pathogens are specifically the ones that make you ill.
writeBullets: Pathogen = a microorganism that causes infectious disease
Four types: bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists
Bacteria examples: Salmonella, Neisseria gonorrhoeae
Virus examples: measles, HIV, tobacco mosaic virus (TMV)
Fungi examples: athlete's foot, rose black spot
Protist examples: Plasmodium (malaria parasite)

keyTerms.def: A microorganism that causes infectious disease
A disease caused by a pathogen that can be transmitted from one person (or organism) to another
Single-celled prokaryotic microorganisms — some cause disease by releasing toxins
Non-cellular pathogens that replicate inside host cells, causing cell damage
Eukaryotic organisms — some are pathogens causing diseases like athlete's foot
Single-celled eukaryotic microorganism — Plasmodium is a protist that causes malaria

keyPoints.memTip: BVFP — Bacteria, Viruses, Fungi, Protists. Make a quick table from memory: four rows, two columns (type + example disease). Do it three times. The examples will stick.

keyPoints.heading: How pathogens spread — the five routes
keyPoints.content: Before a pathogen can make you ill, it has to get inside you. AQA expects you to know five main routes of transmission. Direct contact — touching infected skin or surfaces (e.g. athlete's foot). Airborne droplets — coughing or sneezing releases droplets carrying pathogens, which you breathe in (e.g. measles, influenza). Contaminated water — swallowing water containing pathogens (e.g. cholera). Contaminated food — eating food that contains pathogens or their toxins (e.g. Salmonella in undercooked chicken). Vectors — another organism carries the pathogen to you without being ill itself; mosquitoes are the classic example, carrying malaria's Plasmodium from infected humans to new ones.
writeBullets: Direct contact — touching infected skin/surfaces
Airborne droplets — coughing/sneezing (e.g. measles)
Contaminated water — swallowing water with pathogens (e.g. cholera)
Contaminated food — eating infected food (e.g. Salmonella)
Vectors — organism carries pathogen to new host (e.g. mosquito → malaria)

keyTerms.def: The passing of a pathogen from one host to another
An organism that carries and transmits a pathogen without being ill itself — e.g. mosquitoes carry malaria

keyPoints.memTip: DACFV — Direct contact, Airborne, Contaminated water, Food, Vectors. For each one, write one real example next to it. A disease name turns an abstract route into something memorable.

keyPoints.heading: How bacteria cause disease
keyPoints.content: Bacteria are living cells, so once they're inside your body they do what all cells do: reproduce. They divide rapidly — doubling every 20–30 minutes under ideal conditions — so a small number quickly becomes millions. But reproduction alone isn't what makes you ill. Most of your symptoms are caused by toxins that bacteria release. Salmonella bacteria, for example, don't destroy your gut cells directly — they release toxins that cause fever, vomiting, and diarrhoea. It's the toxins, not the bacteria themselves, that trigger most of your immune response and most of your symptoms. Antibiotics can kill bacteria, which is why bacterial diseases are often treatable — but they only work on bacteria, not viruses.
writeBullets: Bacteria reproduce rapidly inside the body
Bacteria release toxins that damage cells and cause symptoms
Most symptoms (fever, vomiting) are caused by toxins, not bacteria directly
Antibiotics can kill bacteria — they do NOT work on viruses

keyTerms.def: A poison released by bacteria that damages cells and causes disease symptoms
A drug that kills bacteria or stops them reproducing — does not work on viruses

keyPoints.memTip: Bacteria = reproduce fast + release TOXINS. The exam mark is nearly always on the toxin. Write this sentence: 'Bacteria reproduce rapidly and release toxins that damage cells and cause symptoms.' Cover it and write it from memory. That sentence is a mark-earner.

keyPoints.heading: How viruses cause disease
keyPoints.content: Viruses are not cells — they have no metabolism of their own. Instead they're essentially instructions wrapped in a protein coat. To replicate, they hijack a host cell: they get inside, take over the cell's machinery, and force it to make thousands of copies of the virus. When those copies are ready, they burst out of the cell, destroying it in the process. That cell death — multiplied across millions of cells — is what causes symptoms. A single virus particle can infect one cell; by the time your body notices, vast numbers of cells may already be dead. This is why viruses spread through the body so quickly and why they're harder to treat than bacteria — there are no 'anti-virus' drugs equivalent to antibiotics for most viruses.
writeBullets: Viruses hijack host cells — take over the cell's replication machinery
Thousands of virus copies made inside one host cell
Viruses burst out of (and destroy) the host cell
Cell death across many cells causes symptoms
Antibiotics do NOT work on viruses — viruses are not cells

keyTerms.def: The cell a virus infects and uses to replicate itself
The process by which viruses make copies of themselves inside a host cell

keyPoints.memTip: Virus = invade, copy, BURST. Three words, three steps. The burst = cell death = symptoms. Contrast this with bacteria: bacteria reproduce outside cells and poison you with toxins. Viruses destroy you from the inside. That contrast is exam gold.

1.question: Which of the following correctly describes a pathogen?
options: Any microorganism found in the human body
A microorganism that causes infectious disease
A chemical released by bacteria that damages cells
A type of white blood cell that fights infection
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — a pathogen is specifically a microorganism that causes infectious disease. Not all microorganisms are pathogens.
1.wrongFeedback: A pathogen is a microorganism that causes infectious disease. 'Any microorganism' is too broad — most bacteria in your gut are harmless. Toxins and white blood cells are different things entirely.

3.question: A student says: 'Bacteria make you ill by bursting out of your cells.' What is wrong with this statement?
options: Nothing — this is how bacteria cause disease
Bacteria cause disease by releasing toxins, not by bursting out of cells — that's how viruses work
Bacteria cause disease by attacking white blood cells directly
Bacteria only cause disease in plants, not humans
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Good catch — the 'bursting out of cells' mechanism belongs to viruses, not bacteria. Bacteria reproduce rapidly and release toxins that cause symptoms.
3.wrongFeedback: This is a classic mix-up. Bacteria cause disease by reproducing rapidly and releasing toxins. It's viruses that hijack host cells and burst out of them. Keep these two mechanisms separate — AQA will test exactly this distinction.

commonMistakes: Mixing up how bacteria and viruses cause disease — bacteria release toxins, viruses hijack and burst out of cells. These are two different mechanisms and AQA tests both.
Saying antibiotics work on viruses — they don't. Antibiotics only work on bacteria. Writing this in the exam costs you marks every time.
Forgetting that 'pathogen' means infectious disease specifically — not all microorganisms are pathogens. Gut bacteria, for example, are beneficial.
Confusing vector with the disease itself — the mosquito is the vector for malaria; Plasmodium is the actual pathogen. They are not the same thing.
Describing the toxin as the pathogen — the toxin is what the bacterium releases; the bacterium itself is the pathogen. Examiners look for precise language.
examTips: When asked how bacteria cause disease, use two steps: (1) bacteria reproduce rapidly, (2) they release toxins that damage cells and cause symptoms. Both steps, both marks.
When asked how viruses cause disease: (1) they invade/hijack host cells, (2) replicate inside them, (3) burst out — destroying the cell. The cell destruction causing symptoms is the key point.

If an exam question asks you to name the type of pathogen for a named disease, recall the table: Salmonella/gonorrhoea = bacteria; measles/HIV/TMV = virus; malaria = protist (via Plasmodium); athlete's foot = fungus.
revisionCardBullets: Pathogen = microorganism that causes infectious disease
Four types: bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists — know an example disease for each
Bacteria cause disease by: reproducing rapidly + releasing TOXINS that damage cells
Viruses cause disease by: hijacking host cells → replicating → bursting out (cell death)
Antibiotics kill bacteria — they do NOT work on viruses
Five transmission routes: direct contact, airborne droplets, contaminated water, contaminated food, vectors
Vector = organism that carries a pathogen without being ill (e.g. mosquito → malaria)
Plasmodium is the protist pathogen that causes malaria; the mosquito is the vector
Toxin = poison released by bacteria — most bacterial symptoms are caused by toxins, not the bacteria directly
